words: ALFRED ERICKSON: My folks come from Minnesota in the year of 1892. And they come
out because my mother had sent out money to her nephew to buy this place which
belonged to a fellow by the name of Kahn. I don't remember his first name.
Because Kahn was a Civil War veteran and he didn't care to stay back there
in that snow belt you know, and he wanted to get out of there so heAwanted to
sell.Anyway, John Berg, my mother's nephew, had a place right beside the Hog
Meadows, on the east side of the Hog Meadows. John Berg's place. Well he made
the deal. My dad didn't have a cent. In that property. It was ill my mother's
money. And anyway,the old man, he was just about, he was telling about, that
he was about to leave Ma at the station.No, on the train.He was about to jump
off of the train after the train got started. Coming out west. And ma had to
talk to him and get him kind of cooled down. So she finally got him cooled
enough that he want and sat down in the seat. Set there til they come clear to
Troy.
MAY LeMARR: I didn't know that.

speaker: AE
words: Yeah. Well I wasn't born yet at that time, I was born in 1893. They'd been there

speaker: AE
words: a year. And then they had to , the house that I was born in, it was sitting

speaker: AE
words: way over on the north side. And on the west side, northwest side of the place.

speaker: AE
words: And that had, they had a fireplace in the back. I remember that log house.It

speaker: AE
words: had a fireplace just like that, only it was made out of rock. And then they had

speaker: AE
words: their big room here, ^ou know, this was the big end. Then in between, then at

speaker: AE
words: the next end was ^ barn. They had a place there big enough for two horses

speaker: AE
words: and a couple of cows.Then they had a haymow right up over it. Well they used

speaker: AE
words: this middle place to pile up wood in. To keep it dry so that it was built

speaker: AE
words: something like what they had up at Bovill then.That Warren's built on that

speaker: AE
words: meadow up there. It was built in here, you know, log house and then over here

speaker: AE
words: another log house. Then they had the full roof over the both places, don't

speaker: AE
words: you see? So it made it pretty nice. Well anyway, they only lived there about

speaker: AE
words: three or four years, then they decided they were going to build a house. So

speaker: AE
words: they went to work and they built a house, the folks. That is, my dad help

speaker: AE
words: you know. And then they built the house. Then after the house was built, then

speaker: AE
words: we moved down there. Well the place up where I was born, that sit there til

speaker: AE
words: practicallyArotted down. Well, the old man tore the bam down 'cause that

speaker: AE
words: stuff ran out in the field. And we had a field right up on that side hill there.

speaker: AE
words: So we tore that down so we could make a bigger field. Just about where the

speaker: AE
words: plant is there. Remember they used to have the plane?

speaker: AE
words: LENA JUSTICE: Yeah.

speaker: AE
words: God, we used to grow some wonderful pie plants there. Rhubarb, you know. Well

speaker: AE
words: anyway, there was a lot of work there. There was no fence. Kahn didn't have

speaker: AE
words: no cattle, didn't have no horses. So he just let the sheep men run over his

speaker: AE
words: place and they didn't give him nothing. But he didn't care. And he was there,

speaker: AE
words: hefAgur^ on staying there til he proved up. But then he got disgusted and

speaker: AE
words: thought he would just sell out and get five hundred dollars. That's what the

speaker: AE
words: folks put up. And that was too much money.

speaker: LS
words: How many acres was that?

speaker: AE
words: 136 acres. There was a fraction in that place there. So that's what my dad was

speaker: AE
words: awfully disappointed. I couldn't blame him.Into a wood country like that there,

speaker: AE
words: just timber and hard winters.You had to go out pret'near every day or you would

speaker: AE
words: be snowed a road. Well, anyway, finally got a fence made around

speaker: AE
words: the place there and then he got to plowing up a little of the meadow. Put it

speaker: AE
words: into timothy, clover, we used to raise some wonderful wild hay there. Gee whiz,

speaker: AE
words: beautiful wild hay. Then the old man got thafi so, then he built a big barn.

speaker: AE
words: He built a barn that will hald about 35,40 ton of hay. And you know, the hay

speaker: AE
words: was always saleable in the wintertime there. £ know a lot of fellows around there

speaker: AE
words: had a lot of cattle. So the old man could always sell a bunch of hay there.

speaker: AE
words: He never was stuck with it. So he made pretty good on the hay. Then he done a

speaker: AE
words: little work on the county road. He worked on that, but you know, in later years

speaker: AE
words: it become a state road, don't you know. State highway. That was another pretty

speaker: AE
words: good thing. Usually get in about three months work in the summertime on the

speaker: AE
words: roads, what they call the bounty roads. Built by the county. My dad was the

speaker: AE
words: one that helped build the road into Collins. NOt into Collins, but into the

speaker: AE
words: Warren Meadow. Bovill Meadows. Now the road used to go around by what they

speaker: AE
words: called the Horse Ranch. That goes south of Bovill and then it goes to the forks

speaker: AE
words: of the Potlatch. You know where the forks of the Potlatch is? Well at the

speaker: AE
words: forks of the Potlatch was a family living there by the name of Carlson. Carlson

speaker: AE
words: was the first man to run the post office up there. And he named it Janesville.

speaker: AE
words: That was established as Janesville. Well, he run that post office for several

speaker: AE
words: . It made it for my folks to go,'cause it was quite a ways over

speaker: AE
words: that to the forks of the Potlatch. So ...

speaker: LS
words: Was there anything else to Janesville besides the post office?

speaker: AE
words: Well they didn't have a ston, there. But I'll get around to that. Then after

speaker: AE
words: a couple of years, then the post office was changed to Hog Meadows. To Heinsley.

speaker: AE
words: Heinsley owned that,main part of the meadow. And he built a nice big house

speaker: AE
words: there and he had the biggest barn too. Well, then he decided that he would

speaker: AE
words: build on a little store building, right next to the house on the Hog Meadows

speaker: AE
words: there. Heinsley. Make it look like a store, don't you know. You know how they

speaker: AE
words: used to make them fronts on the stores there. Well, he wanted to start in a

speaker: AE
words: little grocery store there. Which he did. And got the post office, well then

speaker: AE
words: that was a little money coming in^all the time. And then he put in a few

speaker: AE
words: groceries, well there was a lot of^homesteaders around in there, they probably

speaker: AE
words: didn't have so far to go for their groceries you see. That made it nice. So

speaker: AE
words: then Heinsley had that til the time  got ready to sell out. And when it was

speaker: AE
words: ksold out, then Lawrence, G.R. Lawrence, the other side of Helmer, he wanted to

speaker: AE
words: get the Janesville post office. So they moved it from there to Lawrence's place. And

speaker: AE
words: then Russ Lawrence

speaker: AE
words: built the store too. So he got in a bunch of groceries and he done pretty good

speaker: AE
words: in the store business there. And that's the way it went. And the last place

speaker: AE
words: that Janesville wounded up with Lawrences. Then they changed post office and

speaker: AE
words: then they put it up at Helmer. There is where they moved the post office but

speaker: AE
words: then it was changed to the name of Helmer. Helmer post office. Janesville was

speaker: AE
words: done away with down.at Lawrences.

speaker: LS
words: Janesville moved three times?

speaker: AE
words: Well that's once, it moved over there once. To Marshall's twice. Janesville

speaker: AE
words: at Lawrences was three times.Three times it moved. So but I don't remember, I

speaker: AE
words: remember when the old man would drive down there to Janesville there at the

speaker: AE
words: Hog Meadows and get the mail, I remember that. But outside of that, but the

speaker: AE
words: people, it would be no use to mention the people living there on these places

speaker: AE
words: would it?

speaker: LS
words: Oh yeah. Mention the early homesteaders.

speaker: LS
words: Shouldn't you go along as the early kids come along?

speaker: AE
words: That wouldn't be necessary, would it? Children being born.

speaker: LS
words: I'm thinking of the homesteaders that actually came there.

speaker: LJ
words: Brothers and sisters he has.

speaker: LS
words: I thought maybe we could get homesteaders first and then get into the family.

speaker: Unidentifed Voice
words: About the people that were living around there that were shopping

speaker: Unidentifed Voice
words: and getting their mail in that Janesville and then over at the other place,

speaker: Unidentifed Voice
words: Helmer, and then Lawrences, the people that lived around All of the people

speaker: Unidentifed Voice
words: that homesteaded and come in there at that time, when they come in.

speaker: LS
words: Just around Hog Meadows there.

speaker: AE
words: Well now, the Hog Meadows that I was telling you, there were three people living

speaker: AE
words: there. There was Heinsley, there was Marshall, there was Hopkins, there was the

speaker: AE
words: three that lived on the Hog Meadows.And John Berg, my cousin, he was on the

speaker: AE
words: east end of the meadows. That's, like if you've come from Helmer up through

speaker: AE
words: Hog Meadows. That's a pretty, long,big meadow there. Well, it's right up in that

speaker: AE
words: east corner where my cousin homesteaded, John Berg. Now I don't think there

speaker: AE
words: would be, well I forgot to name some of those living way back in the woods.

speaker: AE
words: Tnen the other people that right around where we lived there was it began with

speaker: AE
words: was Jim Kelly, he was on the north end. Jim Kelly. Austin Justice,he was on

speaker: AE
words: the north side too,but on the west. And now I don't know about the King Meadows,

speaker: AE
words: they claim there was a fellow by the name of King that homesteaded down the

speaker: AE
words: King Meadows. I don't know if that's true. I don't know as anybody know anything

speaker: AE
words: about that.

speaker: LJ
words: How about Acres?

speaker: AE
words: Acree, he come in there last, don't you know. He come in there last. Well there's

speaker: AE
words: one, two three.

speaker: LJ
words: Tom Shane?

speaker: AE
words: Tom Shane, didn't ownjland. He just rented. Theodore Fold was the owner. And

speaker: AE
words: Theodore Fold bought it from Cook. That's the man that owned the Shea Meadows.

speaker: AE
words: And no, Shea was there for about 4,5, 6 years. And rented from Fold. Fold was

speaker: AE
words: the owner. And our place was the only place up there that never had a mortgage.

speaker: AE
words: And never had a mortgage on our place. All the other places had mortgage except

speaker: AE
words: Acree.

speaker: LJ
words: How 'bout the Bronsons?

speaker: AE
words: Well Bronson was owned by Bronson there you know. His place, you remember that

speaker: AE
words: fence up there at the Bronson as you're coming out on the meadow. Well that

speaker: AE
words: was the line right there. Of Bronson that homesteaded there,you know he come

speaker: AE
words: down to the old man, joining the old man you know.

speaker: LS
words: Do you know how Hog Meadows got its name?

speaker: AE
words: Yeah. Fellow name of Nichols that live now, and I see in this book, the Galloway

speaker: AE
words: brothers was the ones that go up the biggest herd of hags in there to root

speaker: AE
words: the camas out of the ground on that Hog Meadows. But my dad said that a fellow

speaker: AE
words: by the name of Nichols, and he lived down on Texas Ridge, he was the fellow

speaker: AE
words: that brought the hogs in there. YOu see, that's mixed up too. My dad...

speaker: LS
words: It got its name because he brought in a big herd of hogs?

speaker: AE
words: Yeah, he put two or three hundred head of hogs up there. Let them summer up

speaker: AE
words: there and work on that, you know, that camas grew just like an onion. God, it

speaker: AE
words: grows that bih.And it's pretty good to eat stuff.The Indians lived on that

speaker: AE
words: they made bread out of that.

speaker: UV
words: They eat the root and the fruit too?

speaker: AE
words: Yeah. Well they grow like an onion. They had it growing on to^p on it. Prettiest

speaker: AE
words: flower you ever seen. Purple-blue. That was sure fine. I wish that camas was

speaker: AE
words: there, by gosh, I'd go and root out some, (laughs)

speaker: LS
words: Did you eat that a lot as kids?

speaker: L
words: I tasted it.

speaker: ML
words: It's kind of greasy.

speaker: AE
words: It's kind of a gummy like, it wasn't what you'd call good to eat. But then,

speaker: AE
words: if a person knew how to fix the Indians knew how to fix it, how to cook

speaker: AE
words: it. They baked it. And it made good bread

speaker: LS
words: Would you see Indians up there?

speaker: ML
words: Oh yes.

speaker: LJ
words: Used to camp on our folks' place. Early in the morning they'd be out there

speaker: LJ
words: digging camas.(laughs)Dad had to chase them off many a times.

speaker: LS
words: Would you kids go out and play with the m?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yeah, we'd go and see 'em. We'd go to their place, their tipis and they

speaker: LJ
words: even offered us something to eat.

speaker: ML
words: I don't remember that. That was before my time, I guess.

speaker: LJ
words: You were...

speaker: ML
words: The only that I remember about Indians, they come place and get water

speaker: ML
words: from our well. Theu had one of these canvas sacks, they'd fill that. And get

speaker: ML
words: water. And they would go.

speaker: UV
words: When I was young, I can remember siting on an apple box on a table and my

speaker: UV
words: grandmother was out milking the cows and she come in with two buckeTfi of milk,

speaker: UV
words: like this, and you could see her tromping way down the window there up to the

speaker: UV
words: house and there'd be snow that deep, you know. And the first thing, she'd come

speaker: UV
words: in, she'd strain milk and give me a big glass of warm milk to drink.(laughs)

speaker: UV
words: I was an orphan.

speaker: AE
words: Well that's about all I know to tell about the thing out there.

speaker: LJ
words: Go on to the Bovills.

speaker: ML
words: They were pretty famous people.

speaker: LJ
words: They started a store up there.

speaker: AE
words: Yeah, at Bovill, of course Warrens didn't have no store.They didn't even have

speaker: AE
words: a post office there. So of course, Bovill, they  were from rich people. They

speaker: AE
words: were from high society in England. You know of Mrs. Bovill was related to the

speaker: AE
words: king of England? You seen the picture of here, didn't you?

speaker: LS
words: Was he a count?

speaker: UV
words: Remittance man.

speaker: ML
words: I should have brought my bookAI guess for reference about the Bovills.

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember them personally?

speaker: AE
words: Oh yeah, because we traded there.

speaker: LS
words: You'd go up there and trade?

speaker: AE
words: Oh yes. We traded there quite a bit.

speaker: LS
words: What kind of store did they have?

speaker: AE
words: Grocery. Some dry goods. Such as men's clothing, shoes.

speaker: LJ
words: Yardage.

speaker: UV
words: Tea and coffee and sugar,

speaker: LJ
words: Bovill was quite a place at one time too.

speaker: AE
words: Sure after they started in town, why then it built up to quite a ...

speaker: LJ
words: Probably wouldn't think it now, would you?

speaker: AE
words: But it's God dang dead looking now, don't you know it?The town of Bovill is

speaker: AE
words: a fright. Well, you can imagine the amount of people that was working there for

speaker: AE
words: the Potlatch Lumber Company. That's over 1200 to 1500 men there year 'round,

speaker: AE
words: That there is quite a payroll around there.But now, God a man would starve to

speaker: AE
words: death there now. Was you in many of the places when you was up there, Bill?

speaker: AE
words: Go in the taverns? Let's see, there's two taverns there. No, there's three.

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember the town going in there or were you out on the homestead?

speaker: AE
words: Well we stayed on the homestead there. Well, when I got old enough I'd go out

speaker: AE
words: and work somewhere too. I even worked in the logging camps. I went into the

speaker: AE
words: logging camps flunkying one time at the age of about 17,18 years of age.

speaker: LS
words: When you were growing up on the homestead, would you tell me what it was like

speaker: LS
words: living there? How many more sisters and brothers were there?

speaker: ML
words: Four girls...

speaker: AE
words: Four girls and two boys. I'm the only one left.

speaker: L
words: Of the boys.

speaker: AE
words: Think I'm doing pretty good for an old devil.(laughs)

speaker: UV
words: You wuldn't have been around this long if you hadn't been a devil.(laughter)

speaker: ML
words: One thing, the winters were severe. You'd be snowed in in the winter. Except

speaker: ML
words: when you go through and break the road. Take a team of horses and break the

speaker: ML
words: road throggh to the main road. Winters were long and severe.

speaker: LS
words: Could you get out to go to school at all?

speaker: ML
words: Our school was interupted a lot, due to weather conditions.

speaker: AE
words: You know, May and Mollie both went out and boarded some place, some time, they

speaker: AE
words: worked for their board.

speaker: ML
words: Worked for the room and board and that's where I got most of my education, the

speaker: ML
words: little I got.

speaker: LJ
words: I could name the months that I've gone, I guess.

speaker: AE
words: But there's very little schooling that we ever got.

speaker: LS
words: How far away was the school?

speaker: AE
words: Three miles. They called it three miles of that, it was at the lower end of

speaker: AE
words: that Hog Meadows.

speaker: AE
words: When I started to school

speaker: AE
words: You started at Helmer, didn't you?

speaker: AE
words: I started at Helmer.

speaker: AE
words: We started in then Deary, I started at Deary. We had to rent an apartment and

speaker: AE
words: remain away from home.

speaker: LJ
words: That was during the first world war when my brother was in service. We were

speaker: LJ
words: really, it was a Tough , I'm telling you.

words: They had to wrap their feet with gunny sacks.

speaker: ML
words: That's no joke. I saw my brother do that, my younger brother

speaker: AE
words: Had to put gunny sack to keep the toes from freezing off. My god, it was terrinle

speaker: AE
words: cold.

speaker: LJ
words: You didn't do it in Deary, May.

speaker: ML
words: No, we didn't do it in Deary, but I've seen that at home.

speaker: LJ
words: I've done it at home though.

speaker: UV
words: Wrap their feet in gunny sacks and pour water on it and let if freeze so they

words: could keep from freezing to death.

speaker: LS
words: You really did that?

speaker: LJ

words: we put 'em outside of our shoes, gunny sacks. And when they wore out underneath,

words: we'd turn 'em over. Turn them over, put 'em on the other side.

speaker: UV
words: They think they have it hard, nowadays.

speaker: LJ
words: That's right.

speaker: AE
words: Well, you had to go dressed like an Eskimo, (laughs)

speaker: ML
words: We didn't have things to dress as warm as Eskimos,that was it.

speaker: LJ
words: We didn*t have buffalo hides or anything like that.

speaker: ML
words: I should say not. Money was hard to get. Taxes.had to be paid and we alwyas

speaker: ML
words: had to have feed for our stock.That was the two things you had to do.

speaker: LJ
words: What year was that May, when he was in the service?

speaker: ML
words: That'd be in 1918. 1917.

words: We run out of hay and we had quite a few head of cattle, Emil, that's our

words: brother, he bought some hay up at Shea Meadows there. Thirty six dollar a ton.

speaker: LJ
words: Seemed like that's what it was.

speaker: ML
words: It was terrific.

speaker: LJ
words: Anyway, we had an awful time getting that hay home.That was breaking in the

words: spring and the horses go through. When it starts to rain and the snow is kind

words: of crusty. We had...

speaker: ML
words: Merneath the water would be running and the horses would break th rough the

words: snow.

speaker: LJ
words: I think it was, really.

speaker: LJ
words: ML; It was quite...

speaker: LJ
words: Lot of snow.

speaker: AE
words: Yeah, sometimes the winters were not so hard. Then other times, god almighty...

words: I think we measured it on the flat, was it that year, it was higher than my

words: head, anyway. We dug clear down to the ground just to see how deep it was.

speaker: ML
words: We got a lot of snow.

speaker: LJ
words: So our stock come out kind of thin in the spring,

words: Yes they did.

words: About what?

speaker: LJ
words: kids.

words: I don't see anything iiew about it.

words: It's old, that's why it's interesting.

words: Tell about how you helped out.

speaker: AE
words: Well in the morning in the wintertime, well you had to get out and feed.

speaker: UV
words: How many of you done this?

speaker: AE
words: Well I and Emil, when he got big enough, I and Emil and the old man, we'd all

words: go out there. And then'we'd have to cut wood. Jiminy Christmas, we had two

words: stoves agoing. Usually the wood cookstove and the heat stove. And you use

words: it was cold when they start to snap.

words: It burns I guess, when it gets to a certain temperature.

words: you could hear them whenlftiey go up on that snow. Funny noise. It d be so cold

words: it'd make the snow powdery.

speaker: AE
words: Well, you know, they were pretty small, you know. May was the baby, Mollie...)

words: No.
